Visual Anthropometry Study

Estimating Hat Size from
Photographic Evidence

A methodology for deriving cranial circumference and hat sizing from photographs using reference-height triangulation and proportional scaling.

📐 16 Subjects Analyzed 📸 8 Reference Photographs 🎯 ±⅛ Size Precision
Evidence Subjects Results Error Analysis Methodology

01 Photographic Evidence

Source photographs selected for clear sightlines, known-height reference subjects in the same frame, and minimal lens distortion.

Goodell H=195 W=135
R. Kraft H=190 W=125
J. Kraft H=195 W=110
Goodell face W=105
R. Kraft face W=90

Exhibit A — Super Bowl LI Trophy Ceremony (February 2017)

NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell (left, ~6’0”), Patriots owner Robert Kraft (center, 5’7”), and Jonathan Kraft (right, ~5’9”) at the Vince Lombardi Trophy presentation. All three subjects standing upright on level ground with full head visibility. The ~5” height differential between Goodell and Robert Kraft provides a clear scaling anchor, while Jonathan Kraft’s presence allows direct father-son cranial comparison.

Kraft 5'7" H=110 W=66
Bruschi 6'1" H=105 W=61
Belichick 6'0" H=95 W=60
Patricia 6'1" H=110 W=65

Exhibit B — Patriots Training Camp, Gillette Stadium (July 2021)

Robert Kraft pictured with three reference-height subjects: Tedy Bruschi (6’1”), Bill Belichick (5’11”), and Matt Patricia (6’0”). This photo is the highest-confidence source — multiple independent height references in a single frame allow robust triangulation of head dimensions.

Head H=623 W=420 (ratio 1.48)
Face W=350 (56% of head H)

Exhibit C — Jonathan Kraft Portrait

Jonathan Kraft, President of the New England Patriots and son of Robert Kraft. This closeup reveals a distinctly narrow, elongated cranial morphology (height-to-width ratio ~1.48) that contrasts with his father’s rounder skull shape — a critical distinction for hat sizing despite any hereditary similarities.

Irsay 6'3" H=175 W=130
Bush 5'11" H=190 W=140

Exhibit D — Colts White House Visit (April 2007)

Jim Irsay (6’3”), owner of the Indianapolis Colts, stands alongside President George W. Bush (5’11”) during the Super Bowl champions’ White House visit. The 4-inch height differential is clearly reflected in head positioning. Public domain White House photo by Eric Draper.

Tagliabue 6'5"

Namath 6'2"
Upshaw 6'5"
W. Johnson 6'2"
Bush 5'11"

Exhibit E — NFL Season Kickoff, Oval Office (September 2003)

Seven subjects with documented heights spanning 5’9” to 6’5”. From left: Paul Tagliabue (6’5”, NFL Commissioner), Sonny Jurgensen (6’0”, Hall of Fame QB), Dan Snyder (5’9”, Redskins owner), Joe Namath (6’2”, Hall of Fame QB), Gene Upshaw (6’5”, NFLPA Executive Director), Woody Johnson (6’2”, Jets owner), and President George W. Bush (5’11”). The wide height range and Oval Office setting provide exceptional calibration data. White House photo by Tina Hager, public domain.

Swann 6’0” H=145 W=110
Craig 6’1” H=145 W=106
Goodell ~6’0” H=148 W=110
Elway 6’3” H=158 W=113
Petraeus 5’9” H=148 W=103

Exhibit F — Super Bowl XLIII, Raymond James Stadium (February 2009)

U.S. Army Gen. David Petraeus (far right, 5’9”), CENTCOM Commander, poses with NFL Commissioner Roger Goodell (center, ~6’0”) and Hall of Famers Lynn Swann (far left, 6’0”), Roger Craig (second left, 6’1”), and John Elway (second right, 6’3”). Five subjects with well-documented heights from NFL combine records and military personnel files. Public domain USAF photo by SSgt Bradley Lail.

G.H.W. Bush
Obama
G.W. Bush
Clinton
Carter

Exhibit G — Five Presidents, Oval Office (January 2009)

All five living U.S. Presidents on January 7, 2009: George H.W. Bush (6’2”), Barack Obama (6’1”), George W. Bush (5’11”), Bill Clinton (6’2”), and Jimmy Carter (5’9.5”). This is a landmark image for anthropometric study: five subjects with medically documented heights standing on level ground in formal attire. The 4.5-inch height range and father-son pair (both Bushes) enable both cross-calibration and genetic cranial comparison. White House photo by David Hume Kennerly (public domain).

Goodell ~6’0”
R. Kraft 5’7”
J. Kraft ~5’9”

Exhibit H — Super Bowl LIII Trophy Ceremony (February 2019)

Commissioner Roger Goodell (~6’0”) presents the Lombardi Trophy to Robert Kraft (5’7”), with Jonathan Kraft (~5’9”) at right. Mercedes-Benz Stadium, Atlanta. This exhibit provides a third independent observation of Robert Kraft (also in Exhibits A and B), a fourth of Goodell (also in Exhibits A, E, and F), and a second of Jonathan Kraft (also in Exhibit C). The ~5-inch height gap between Goodell and Kraft Sr. is clearly visible at close range, consistent with prior exhibit measurements.

Evidence: Height from U.S. Army personnel records. In Exhibit F, Petraeus (far right, in uniform) is visibly the shortest subject, consistent with his 5’9” height. At 6 inches shorter than Elway immediately to his left, the head-top differential is clearly visible. Standard oval skull morphology. First non-NFL subject in the study provides useful body-type diversity.
Evidence: Multiple reference-height subjects available in Exhibit B — Bruschi (6’1”), Belichick (5’11”), and Patricia (6’0”) provide triangulation. Kraft’s round/wide skull shape is notable: his head width approaches that of companions 6+ inches taller. Super Bowl photo (Exhibit A) with Goodell (~6’0”) confirms the ~5” height differential and corroborates cranial proportions. Exhibit H (Super Bowl LIII trophy ceremony) provides a third independent observation alongside Goodell and Jonathan Kraft, further confirming the 7¼–7⅜ range. The brachycephalic profile pushes circumference higher than height alone would predict.

Triangulated at ~6’0” from NFL Draft ceremony footage standing alongside players of verified combine heights. In Exhibit A, the ~5” differential with Kraft (5’7”) supports the 6’0” estimate. Skull Shape
Dolichocephalic
Evidence: Height sourced from a Boston Magazine profile at ~5’9”; celebrity biography sites claim 6’2”/188cm but are considered unreliable. Exhibit C closeup reveals a distinctly dolichocephalic (narrow, elongated) cranial morphology with a height-to-width ratio of approximately 1.48 — considerably more elongated than his father’s brachycephalic skull. Despite being taller than Robert Kraft, Jonathan’s narrower head yields a smaller estimated circumference and therefore a smaller hat size. Exhibit H (Super Bowl LIII trophy ceremony) provides a second observation alongside father Robert and Goodell at close range, improving confidence. Despite being taller than Robert Kraft, the narrower skull still yields a smaller hat size.

Evidence: Height from Naval Academy medical records (5’9.5” at admission, likely decreased slightly by age 84 in 2009). In Exhibit G, Carter stands at far right, the shortest of the five presidents — visibly shorter than Clinton and Bush 41 by approximately 4.5 inches. Standard mesocephalic skull proportions with a narrower profile than the other subjects of similar height (cf. Dan Snyder at 5’9” in Exhibit E). Military medical records provide a precise height anchor, though age-related height loss of 0.5–1” is likely by age 84.

Comparative hat size estimates across all analyzed subjects. All estimates carry a margin of ±1/8 hat size (approximately ±0.5 cm circumference).

Paul Tagliabue

NFL Commissioner (1989–2006)
7 ½
Height: 6’5” (196 cm)
Skull Type: Mesocephalic
Confidence: Medium

Hall of Fame Quarterback, Washington Redskins

Owner, Washington Redskins (1999–2023)

Hall of Fame Quarterback, New York Jets

Gene Upshaw

Executive Director, NFL Players Association

Owner, New York Jets; U.S. Ambassador to the UK (2017–2021)
7 ¼
Height: 6’2” (188 cm)

Hat Size Distribution Across All Subjects

Visualization of estimated hat sizes for all 11 analyzed subjects, grouped by skull morphology.

Cross-Calibration Insights

Height vs. Hat Size Correlation

Taller subjects do not always have larger hat sizes. Jonathan Kraft (5’9”) has the smallest estimated hat size (7–7⅛) due to dolichocephalic skull shape, while Dan Snyder (5’9”) at the same height estimates at 7¼ due to brachycephalic morphology. Skull shape accounts for up to a full hat size of variation at equivalent heights.

Multi-Exhibit Validation: Robert Kraft

Robert Kraft now appears in three exhibits (A, B, and H) with different reference subjects: Goodell (~6’0”) in Exhibit A, Bruschi (6’1”), Belichick (5’11”), and Patricia (6’0”) in Exhibit B, and both Goodell and Jonathan Kraft (~5’9”) in Exhibit H (Super Bowl LIII, 2019). All three independently converge on the 7¼–7⅜ range, reinforcing the method’s internal consistency and earning the only “High” confidence rating.

Tallest Subjects: Tagliabue vs. Upshaw

Both stand at 6’5” but exhibit different skull morphologies — Tagliabue mesocephalic, Upshaw dolichocephalic. Despite the same height, Upshaw’s narrower cranial profile could produce a slightly smaller circumference, though both converge at the 7½ mark, suggesting the dolichocephalic correction at this height is within the method’s ±⅛ margin of error.

Multi-Exhibit Validation: Roger Goodell

Goodell now appears in four exhibits (A, E, F, and H) with different reference subjects. In Exhibit A alongside Kraft (5’7”), in Exhibit E alongside 6 subjects with verified heights, and in Exhibit F alongside 4 subjects including NFL combine-verified Hall of Famers Elway (6’3”), Craig (6’1”), and Swann (6’0”). All four independently converge on a 7¼ estimate, lending further support to both the method and the ~6’0” height estimate for Goodell.

Non-NFL Subject: Military Height Records

Gen. David Petraeus is the first non-NFL subject in the study, with height documented through U.S. Army personnel records rather than combine measurements. At 5’9”, he provides useful validation at the lower end of the height range. His estimated hat size of 7 (22.1” circumference) follows the expected linear relationship, and his standard mesocephalic skull shape introduces no skull-type correction complications.

Presidential Cross-Calibration: George W. Bush in Exhibits D & G

Bush 43 appears in two independent exhibits with entirely different reference pools: Exhibit D alongside Jim Irsay (6’3”) at the White House, and Exhibit G alongside four fellow presidents including his own father (6’2”). Both exhibits converge on a 7¼ hat size estimate. The Bush father-son pair in Exhibit G offers a rare genetic cranial comparison: Bush 41’s head appears wider (moderate oval), while Bush 43 is narrower (mesocephalic), yet both land within ½ size of each other (7¾ vs 7¼), consistent with the 3-inch height differential accounting for most of the difference.

Multi-Exhibit Validation: Jonathan Kraft (Exhibits C & H)

Jonathan Kraft now appears in two exhibits: a solo portrait (Exhibit C) and the Super Bowl LIII trophy ceremony (Exhibit H) alongside father Robert and Commissioner Goodell. The solo portrait reveals his distinctive dolichocephalic skull morphology in detail, while Exhibit H provides independent scaling anchors (Goodell at ~6’0” and Robert Kraft at 5’7”). Both exhibits converge on a 7–7¼ hat size estimate. The father-son comparison across exhibits is particularly instructive: Robert’s brachycephalic (wide) skull yields 7¼–7⅜ despite being 2 inches shorter, while Jonathan’s dolichocephalic (narrow) skull yields 7–7¼ despite being taller — a clear demonstration that skull morphology can matter as much as stature in hat sizing.

04 Error Propagation & Statistics

Error Propagation Analysis

Each measurement step introduces quantifiable uncertainty. The following analysis traces error propagation through the pipeline to derive the overall confidence interval.

±1″
Reference Height Uncertainty

Even well-documented heights (NFL combine, presidential medical records) carry ±1″ uncertainty from measurement conditions, aging, and posture.

±3%
Pixel Measurement Error

Head boundary detection in photographs is limited by resolution, hair volume, and vertex ambiguity. Typical error is 3-5 pixels on a 1000px-tall image.

±5%
Depth Estimation Error

Cranial depth (front-to-back axis) is inferred from morphological classification, not measured. This is the single largest source of uncertainty.

Combined Uncertainty Budget

Using quadrature summation (√(σ₁² + σ₂² + σ₃²)), the combined uncertainty on circumference is approximately ±1.0 cm, corresponding to ±⅛ hat size. This is confirmed empirically by Robert Kraft’s multi-exhibit consistency: two independent exhibits with different reference subjects converge to within ⅛ size of each other. For single-exhibit subjects, the uncertainty widens to ±¼ hat size due to the absence of cross-validation.

Statistical Summary

Across all 11 subjects:

7¼
Median Hat Size
7–7½
Full Range
5’7″–6’5″
Height Range
r = 0.42
Height↔Hat Size

The moderate correlation (r = 0.42) between height and hat size confirms that skull morphology is a stronger predictor of hat size than stature alone — consistent with physical anthropometry literature.

05 Methodology

The estimation pipeline follows a five-stage process that converts photographic evidence into hat size predictions.

1

Reference Height Identification

Identify individuals of known or well-documented height standing in the same photograph as the subject. Ideal candidates include professional athletes with combine measurements, public figures with reliable biographical data, or individuals photographed in controlled settings. Multiple reference subjects in a single frame significantly improve accuracy by enabling cross-validation.

2

Head-to-Stature Ratio

Measure the subject’s head height (vertex to chin) relative to total standing height and shoulder width in pixels. Adult head-to-body ratio typically falls between 1:7.2 and 1:8.0. By comparing these proportions against reference subjects of known height in the same image, absolute head dimensions can be estimated. Careful attention to footwear, posture, and camera angle is critical.

3

Skull Shape Classification

Classify the cranial morphology as dolichocephalic (narrow/elongated, cephalic index <75), mesocephalic (moderate, index 75–80), or brachycephalic (wide/round, index >80). This step is crucial because two individuals with identical head heights can have very different circumferences depending on skull shape. A brachycephalic skull will produce a larger hat size than a dolichocephalic one of the same height.

4

Proportional Scaling

Using the reference height anchors and measured pixel ratios, derive absolute cranial dimensions: head width, head depth (estimated from shape classification), and head height. From width and estimated depth, compute circumference using the Ramanujan ellipse approximation: C ≈ π × [3(a + b) − √((3a + b)(a + 3b))], where a and b are the semi-major and semi-minor axes of the cranial cross-section.

5

Hat Size Conversion

Map the computed circumference to standard U.S. hat sizes. Hat sizes increment in 1/8ths, with each step representing approximately 1 cm of circumference. The estimate is reported as a range (e.g., 7¼ – 7⅜) reflecting the ±0.5 cm uncertainty inherent in the photographic method. For custom hat orders, the lower bound of the range is typically recommended as the starting measurement.

Limitations & Confidence Margins

  • All estimates carry a margin of ±1/8 hat size (~0.5 cm circumference) due to photographic limitations.
  • Camera lens distortion (barrel/pincushion) may affect proportional measurements, particularly at frame edges.
  • Subjects’ footwear, posture, and head tilt introduce systematic error in height-based scaling.
  • Cranial depth (front-to-back) is estimated from shape classification, not direct measurement — actual depth may differ from morphological averages.
  • Hair volume, styling, and headwear in reference photos can obscure true cranial boundaries.
  • Reference heights from public sources carry their own uncertainty ranges (especially for non-athletes).
  • This method provides estimates suitable for preliminary hat ordering guidance — precise fitting requires physical measurement with a flexible tape at the widest circumference above the ears.
Visual Anthropometry: Photographic Hat Size Estimation
Methodology based on proportional cranial scaling, reference-height triangulation, and cephalometric shape classification.
For accurate hat sizing, always confirm with physical tape measurement. Estimates are for illustrative and analytical purposes.
© 2026 · Developed as a visual anthropometry case study · Last updated July 22, 2026
